text: This is an unedited, uncut video of the laparoscopic hysterectomy from the beginning to the end.
The surgeon performs adhesiolysis using a harmonic scalpel to remove dense adhesions from previous caesarean sections, carefully separating the adhered tissues.
The laparoscopic view reveals a bulky uterus with multiple fibroids visible on its surface.
The laparoscopic view shows the sigmoid colon adjacent to the uterus, which has large anterior and posterior fibroids visible.
The surgeon addresses minor adhesions between the ovary and surrounding tissues, carefully separating them.
The fallopian tube is visible in the surgical field, with the ovary located deeper within the pelvic cavity.
The surgeon mobilizes the sigmoid colon to improve exposure of the ureter and adnexal structures. The ovary is visibly adhered to the ovarian fossa and the uterus.
The surgeon begins the hysterectomy procedure, focusing on the bulky uterus with multiple fibroids visible in the surgical field.
In this video we have not cut, we have not edited. The full length videos are very useful for learning.
The surgeon identifies the ureter, which is visible alongside the infundibulopelvic ligament. The ureter is seen ascending in the surgical field.
The surgeon prepares to perform a bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy (BSO), focusing on the removal of both fallopian tubes and ovaries.
The surgeon holds a ligature in the right hand and a bipolar grasper in the left hand, preparing to manipulate and secure tissue structures during the hysterectomy procedure.

The surgeon cuts the round ligament and dissects the fallopian tube. The infundibulopelvic ligament is separated, and both the anterior and posterior leaves of the broad ligament are opened. The uterus is given contralateral traction, and the round ligament on the left side is being separated.
The surgeon identifies the ovary, which appears to be affected by endometriosis, characterized by visible lesions or discoloration on the ovarian surface.
The surgeon identifies the infundibulopelvic ligament and the anterior leaf of the broad ligament, highlighting these anatomical structures within the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a uterine manipulator to push the uterus towards the upper abdomen. The ovary, which is adhered, is carefully separated from the ovarian fossa.

The surgeon pushes the uterus further into the upper abdomen using a uterine manipulator, facilitating the dissection of the round ligament and separation of the anterior leaf of the broad ligament. The uterus is retroverted to aid in the procedure.
The surgeon carefully separates the anterior leaf of the broad ligament, maintaining a medial approach to avoid lateral deviation, especially in the presence of a large uterus.
The surgeon separates the fallopian tube and ovary from the uterus, using coagulation to prevent bleeding and improve visibility in the surgical field.
The surgeon separates the uterovesical fold of the peritoneum on the opposite side of the anterior leaf of the broad ligament, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon separates the bladder from the uterus, encountering a fibroid that obstructs the view. A third-digit telescope is utilized to enhance visualization over the fibroid.
The surgeon encounters difficulty in separating the bladder due to the patient's previous surgical history, which is visually evident as the bladder remains adhered and requires careful dissection.

The surgeon encounters another fibroid, which presents a challenge in separating the tissue. Using a harmonic scalpel, the surgeon performs slow and careful dissection to establish a clear surgical plane.
The surgeon carefully separates the bladder from surrounding tissues to prevent injury to the ureter, navigating through multiple fibroids and adhesions from previous surgeries.
The surgeon elevates the bladder, revealing the vaginal fascia beneath it, which becomes visible as the dissection progresses.
The surgeon advances to further pedicles, revealing a shiny tissue surface. The bladder pillars are visible and can be separated easily.
The uterine artery is visible, and the surgeon uses a ligature to address the lower pedicles, maintaining proximity to the uterus during the laparoscopic total hysterectomy.
The surgeon uses a ligature device to coagulate the uterine artery, with some bleeding visible as collected blood is released. The procedure then progresses to the opposite side.

The surgeon completes the dissection on the right side using an ipsilateral port from the left side, ensuring a clear surgical plane as the bladder has already been separated. The telescope is cleaned to maintain visibility.
The surgeon observes the pulsation of the uterine artery, which is being skeletonized. The uterine artery is coagulated using an electrosurgical device.

The surgeon addresses adhesions on the posterior side caused by a large fibroid. The ligature is brought from the same side to the right side, holding the uterine structure perpendicular, despite typical opposite direction handling due to prior dissection.
The surgeon completes the separation of the bladder, and a full circle colpotomizer is pushed into place, with the ring of the colpotomizer visibly encircling the vaginal cuff.
The surgeon addresses posterior adhesions using a harmonic device, carefully separating the adhesions to improve visibility. The tip of the instrument is cleaned to ensure effective operation.
The surgeon uses a ligature to separate the vein and the macundrat, with the artery already separated in the surgical field.
The surgeon observes potential bleeding at the angles of the vagina, noting that posterior bleeding is more significant due to the presence of veins.
The surgeon begins the colpotomy, using the colpotomizer as a guide. The white colpotomizer is visible, and the vaginal tissue is being incised over its tip.
The surgeon observes the pulsation of the uterine vessels before applying the harmonic or hook laterally, ensuring safer coagulation.

The surgeon carefully maneuvers the harmonic scalpel away from the tip of the colpotomizer to avoid cutting it. The view shifts to the posterior aspect, where the utero sacral ligaments are visible on either side. The cervical part of the utero sacral ligament is being cut, with adhesions present in the area.

The uterine manipulator is adjusted to position the colpotomizer appropriately, facilitating the colpotomy procedure. The surgeon performs the colpotomy from the same side, contrary to the usual practice of accessing from the opposite side.

The assistant pushes the colpotomizer contralaterally to facilitate the colpotomy on the right side. The uterus is then removed, with the option to bisect it vaginally using a speculum. Suturing begins following the removal.

The surgeon uses a number one vicryl suture for intracorporeal suturing, employing a seizure knot technique with a double wrap. The vaginal epithelium is included in the suturing, while the bladder is carefully separated. Continuous suturing is performed with locking stitches.

The surgeon performs wall closure following a total laparoscopic hysterectomy, carefully suturing the lateral angles of the uterine shackle. The procedure requires precise dissection and use of energy sources to ensure safe and effective closure.
The surgeon tightens the knot by holding the suture rather than the needle, ensuring secure closure without needle manipulation during the tightening process.
The surgeon performs irrigation and suction to clean the surgical area, checking for any bleeding and thoroughly examining the pelvis. The ten millimeter ports are closed following the procedure.
